title: Chickpea salad with lemon
subtitle: Tinned chickpeas with cucumber, tomato, and a cumin-lemon dressing.
excerpt: Tinned chickpeas drained and tossed with cucumber, cherry tomatoes, red onion, and flat-leaf parsley in a dressing of lemon, olive oil, cumin, and smoked paprika. No cooking. Ready in ten minutes.
sourceNotes: Chickpea salads appear across the Mediterranean and Middle East, from Levantine fattoush variations to Spanish ensalada de garbanzos. The lemon-and-cumin dressing reflects North African and Middle Eastern influence that entered British home cooking through the wave of Mediterranean cookbooks from the 1980s onwards. Tinned chickpeas made the dish pantry-practical in British kitchens.

content.text: This is a pantry salad: two tins of chickpeas, a lemon, and whatever is in the vegetable drawer. The dressing does most of the work. Cumin and smoked paprika give it more character than a plain vinaigrette, and the lemon keeps it bright rather than heavy.
Rinse the chickpeas thoroughly. The liquid in the tin (aquafaba) tastes fine in cooking but dulls the flavour of a dressing, so drain it off completely.

items.name: Tinned chickpeas
items.prepNote: two 400 g tins, drained and rinsed

content.text: A colander to rinse the chickpeas, a knife and board, and a large mixing bowl.

Whisk {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, the lemon juice, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, and {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika together in a large bowl. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper.

Add the drained chickpeas, cucumber, cherry tomatoes, red onion, and parsley to the dressing. Toss well. Leave for at least 10 minutes before serving so the chickpeas absorb the dressing. Scatter over the feta if using.

items.fix: Add more salt and an extra squeeze of lemon. The cumin and paprika can both be increased, the recipe is conservative to allow for varied palates.
items.cause: Chickpeas from the tin are very neutral and need the full dressing quantity.

items.fix: Soak the sliced onion in cold water for 10 minutes, then drain. This removes sharpness without losing flavour.
items.cause: Raw red onion can be very pungent depending on the variety.

items.fix: Add another tablespoon of olive oil and a squeeze of lemon. The chickpeas will continue to absorb as they sit.
items.cause: Left for more than two hours without refreshing.
items.symptom: Chickpeas have soaked up all the dressing

items.fix: Add more lemon juice to balance. Swap to sweet paprika next time if you prefer a milder flavour.
items.cause: Some smoked paprika is more intensely flavoured than others.

content.text: The British chickpea salad came in with the wave of Mediterranean and Middle Eastern cooking. Writers like Claudia Roden and Yotam Ottolenghi carried it into UK kitchens. The lemon-and-cumin dressing has its roots in North African cooking, where pulses are seasoned with warm spices and a sharp acid. What made the salad a British weeknight staple is the humble tin of chickpeas. Cheap, in every supermarket, ready to use from the tin.
